behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is in the grouping algorithm—either trace IDs are not matched correctly or the tool filters by service before grouping. This is pure deterministic logic. A unit test with mock log entries from multiple services all having the same trace ID fully exercises the grouping logic without needing external infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock log entries from three different microservices (service-a, service-b, service-c) all with trace_id='abc123', pass them through the grouping logic in random order, and verify all entries are grouped under a single trace container with key 'abc123', regardless of arrival order or source service.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test directly exercises the grouping logic with controlled inputs. No external dependencies or non-determinism needed.

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is in the clock skew correction or ordering algorithm. Since timestamps are fields in log entries (not OS-level reads), you can inject mocked timestamps with various skew amounts and verify the ordering algorithm handles them deterministically. This tests the algorithm in isolation without needing real system clocks.
behaviors.test_description: Create mock log entries with timestamps deliberately out of order and clock-skewed (e.g., service-a at T, service-b at T-200ms, service-c at T+500ms). Pass through the timeline builder and verify the output is ordered by corrected logical time, not input order. Test boundaries: exactly 500ms skew is handled; >500ms behavior matches the spec.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test exercises the ordering algorithm with controlled timestamp inputs. No real clock synchronization needed.

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is likely in aggregation or correlation logic: baseline p99 computed on wrong traces, per-trace latency extracted incorrectly, or boundary comparisons broken. These require testing multiple trace entries together to verify baseline computation and comparison are coordinated correctly. A unit test on isolated latency values risks missing failures in how baseline and spike interact across the dataset.
behaviors.test_description: Create a dataset of mock log entries representing multiple complete traces with realistic latency fields or timing events. Compute baseline p99 latency across most traces. Add one trace with end-to-end latency >3x the baseline. Pass all traces through anomaly detection and verify the spiking trace is flagged. Test boundary: 3.0x not flagged, 3.1x flagged. Verify baseline is computed from the correct trace set.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test requires coordinating multiple traces and verifying aggregation logic in context, which is Integration-level work.

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is either the parser crashes on malformed input or the skip counter is not incremented correctly. Both are logic errors in error handling that a unit test can catch by injecting malformed entries and verifying the counter. This doesn't require a real pipeline or streaming context; the parser logic is the failure boundary.
behaviors.test_description: Create a list of log entries where 90% are valid JSON and 10% are intentionally malformed (missing closing brace, invalid escapes, non-string keys, etc.). Pass through the parser. Verify all 90% of valid entries are processed, the skip counter reports exactly 10%, and no uncaught exceptions are raised.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test exercises error handling in the parser with controlled inputs. Small risk that the real failure is in pipeline integration (async exception handling), but primary failure is in parser logic.

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind.
behaviors.justification: The real failure is a performance or reliability issue under load: unbounded memory growth, single-threaded bottleneck, buffer overflow, or crashes. A unit test processes synchronously and won't reveal these. An Integration-level load test with in-memory data and simulated burst (rapid in-process feeding) can catch buffer overflows, memory pressure, and concurrency issues. System-level testing with real I/O might reveal additional bottlenecks, but the stated behavior is testable at Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Create 10,000 log entries in memory and feed them to the tool as rapidly as possible (e.g., in a tight loop, simulating 10,000 entries/sec burst). Measure processing time. Verify (a) output contains all 10,000 entries with no data loss, (b) processing completes in reasonable time (<2 seconds), (c) tool does not crash, hang, or exhaust memory. Include malformed entries in the burst to test robustness under load.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test exercises performance and resilience under burst load in-process. A true System test with real I/O might reveal additional failures, but Integration-level load testing adequately covers the stated behavior.
